bins.name: Large Loads Pay Full Upgrade Costs
bins.desc: Buckeye argues that data centers drive massive transmission investments that, if the load fails to materialize or sustain, will be stranded on uninvolved wholesale customers. Buckeye supports ANOPR Principle 8 requiring large loads to pay 100% of network upgrade costs upfront, with a crediting mechanism only if designed to protect other customers. Fallback options -- holding the host utility responsible or requiring retail protections to flow into wholesale rates -- are also supported.
quotes: There is serious concern that these large loads could drive significant transmission investment and then not show up to pay for it on a sustained basis.

If a large load fails to materialize or consume electricity at the level and duration expected when these transmission investments were made, other wholesale transmission customers (and, ultimately, their retail customers) may be faced with the massive costs of investments they did not cause.

Because the Commission is obligated under the Federal Power Act ("FPA") to ensure that rates under its jurisdiction are just, reasonable, and not unduly discriminatory, the Commission must ensure that large loads are responsible for the cost of transmission facilities built to serve them so other wholesale customers are not left holding the bag.

Buckeye supports holding large loads such as data centers directly responsible for 100% of the network upgrades they are assigned through interconnection studies (which could include crediting, provided that crediting is designed to ensure that wholesale transmission customers are protected).

Although these requirements may prevent speculative projects from entering the study process, they do not address the major stranded cost concerns with data centers that complete the study process, cause massive transmission upgrades, but then do not have the expected level of demand over a sustained period.
Load growth of approximately 10,000 MW driven largely by data centers in the Dominion Zone of PJM resulted in approximately $12 billion in transmission investment across PJM.
While these costs are primarily driven by data center loads, currently all customers in the corresponding PJM transmission zones and, where applicable, in the broader PJM region will bear them.

If the relevant wholesale transmission entity, like AEP Ohio, were responsible for the network upgrade costs caused by their retail customers seeking large load interconnections, that could both protect other wholesale transmission customers and still allow a mechanism for ultimately holding large loads responsible for the costs they cause.

At a minimum (and as a transition mechanism before upfront funding measures take effect, as discussed in Part III.D below), the Commission must ensure that any existing retail-level protections against the risks of stranded costs of Commission-jurisdictional transmission service resulting from large loads will be reflected and accounted for in Commission-jurisdictional rates.
Under AEP Ohio's retail tariff, these measures protect only AEP Ohio's own retail customers from these significant stranded transmission cost risks, leaving wholesale transmission customers exposed.
It is absolutely essential that this Commission address these issues and cost shifting concerns at the federal level.
An appropriate crediting mechanism for this purpose will need to be different from the crediting mechanism used for large generator interconnections.

bins.name: Study Deposits and Transition Coverage
bins.desc: Buckeye supports standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties to deter speculative projects and improve load forecasting. It also supports applying protections to large load interconnections already in the study process as a transition mechanism.
quotes: There is serious concern that these large loads could drive significant transmission investment and then not show up to pay for it on a sustained basis.
Buckeye supports requirements to deter speculative projects, such as standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties.

Although these requirements may prevent speculative projects from entering the study process, they do not address the major stranded cost concerns with data centers that complete the study process, cause massive transmission upgrades, but then do not have the expected level of demand over a sustained period.
Buckeye supports a transition mechanism for the treatment of large load interconnections that are already being studied.

bins.name: Deposits Deter Speculation, Not Stranded Costs
bins.desc: Buckeye supports study deposits and withdrawal penalties as tools to reduce speculative queue entries but is explicit that these measures do not address stranded cost risks that arise after transmission is built and large loads fail to sustain expected demand.
quotes: Buckeye supports requirements to deter speculative projects, such as standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties.

Although these requirements may prevent speculative projects from entering the study process, they do not address the major stranded cost concerns with data centers that complete the study process, cause massive transmission upgrades, but then do not have the expected level of demand over a sustained period.

bins.name: Direct Assignment of Upgrade Costs
bins.desc: Buckeye supports direct assignment of 100% of network upgrade, interconnection facility, and attachment facility costs to large loads. It notes that any crediting mechanism must be designed differently from the generator-interconnection financing model to actually prevent cost shifts to other wholesale customers.
quotes: If a large load fails to materialize or consume electricity at the level and duration expected when these transmission investments were made, other wholesale transmission customers (and, ultimately, their retail customers) may be faced with the massive costs of investments they did not cause.

Buckeye supports holding large loads such as data centers directly responsible for 100% of the network upgrades they are assigned through interconnection studies (which could include crediting, provided that crediting is designed to ensure that wholesale transmission customers are protected).

If the relevant wholesale transmission entity, like AEP Ohio, were responsible for the network upgrade costs caused by their retail customers seeking large load interconnections, that could both protect other wholesale transmission customers and still allow a mechanism for ultimately holding large loads responsible for the costs they cause.
An appropriate crediting mechanism for this purpose will need to be different from the crediting mechanism used for large generator interconnections.

bins.name: FERC Must Act, Not States Alone
bins.desc: Buckeye contends that FERC has a statutory obligation under the FPA to ensure just and reasonable rates, and that state retail tariffs -- like AEP Ohio's -- cannot protect wholesale transmission customers from cost shifts caused by large loads. Federal action at the wholesale level is essential.
quotes: Because the Commission is obligated under the Federal Power Act ("FPA") to ensure that rates under its jurisdiction are just, reasonable, and not unduly discriminatory, the Commission must ensure that large loads are responsible for the cost of transmission facilities built to serve them so other wholesale customers are not left holding the bag.

At a minimum (and as a transition mechanism before upfront funding measures take effect, as discussed in Part III.D below), the Commission must ensure that any existing retail-level protections against the risks of stranded costs of Commission-jurisdictional transmission service resulting from large loads will be reflected and accounted for in Commission-jurisdictional rates.
It is absolutely essential that this Commission address these issues and cost shifting concerns at the federal level.

bins.name: PJM Stranded Cost Exposure
bins.desc: Buckeye draws on PJM data to illustrate the scale of the problem: approximately $12 billion in transmission investment driven by data center load in the Dominion Zone alone, with costs currently socialized across all PJM customers rather than charged to the data centers that caused them.
quotes: Load growth of approximately 10,000 MW driven largely by data centers in the Dominion Zone of PJM resulted in approximately $12 billion in transmission investment across PJM.
While these costs are primarily driven by data center loads, currently all customers in the corresponding PJM transmission zones and, where applicable, in the broader PJM region will bear them.
Under AEP Ohio's retail tariff, these measures protect only AEP Ohio's own retail customers from these significant stranded transmission cost risks, leaving wholesale transmission customers exposed.

bins.name: Billions in Stranded Cost Risk
bins.desc: Buckeye quantifies the transmission investment driven by data center loads -- $12 billion from the Dominion Zone and over $10 billion projected for Central Ohio -- to show that the stranded cost risk is not theoretical but already materializing at a scale that demands federal action.
quotes: Load growth of approximately 10,000 MW driven largely by data centers in the Dominion Zone of PJM resulted in approximately $12 billion in transmission investment across PJM.

bins.name: Costs Spread to Uninvolved Customers
bins.desc: Buckeye opposes the current default outcome in which data-center-driven transmission costs are socialized across all PJM customers. It uses a numerical table showing that if data centers do not sustain forecast demand, other customers in the same transmission zone bear hundreds of millions in costs they did not cause.
quotes: While these costs are primarily driven by data center loads, currently all customers in the corresponding PJM transmission zones and, where applicable, in the broader PJM region will bear them.

bins.name: Hold Host Utility Responsible at Wholesale
bins.desc: If FERC does not directly assign upgrade costs to large loads, Buckeye proposes holding the relevant wholesale transmission entity (the host utility with a retail relationship with the large load) responsible at the wholesale level. That entity can then recover costs from the data center through state retail mechanisms.
quotes: If the relevant wholesale transmission entity, like AEP Ohio, were responsible for the network upgrade costs caused by their retail customers seeking large load interconnections, that could both protect other wholesale transmission customers and still allow a mechanism for ultimately holding large loads responsible for the costs they cause.

bins.name: Retail Protections Must Reach Wholesale Rates
bins.desc: As a minimum fallback, Buckeye argues that any retail tariff provisions (minimum charges, exit fees, etc.) securing stranded cost recovery must be reflected in FERC-jurisdictional wholesale rates. Without this, host utilities double-recover by collecting from both the data center at retail and from wholesale customers through FERC rates.
quotes: At a minimum (and as a transition mechanism before upfront funding measures take effect, as discussed in Part III.D below), the Commission must ensure that any existing retail-level protections against the risks of stranded costs of Commission-jurisdictional transmission service resulting from large loads will be reflected and accounted for in Commission-jurisdictional rates.
Under AEP Ohio's retail tariff, these measures protect only AEP Ohio's own retail customers from these significant stranded transmission cost risks, leaving wholesale transmission customers exposed.

bins.name: Apply Rules to In-Queue Projects
bins.desc: Buckeye supports applying cost protection rules to large load interconnections already in the study queue, not just future filings, because transmission investment to serve those loads is already underway and creating cost-shift risk today.
quotes: Buckeye supports a transition mechanism for the treatment of large load interconnections that are already being studied.

bins.name: Crediting Must Differ from Generator Model
bins.desc: Buckeye notes that generator-interconnection crediting was designed only as a financing mechanism, not to protect other customers from stranded cost risks. Any crediting mechanism for large loads must be structured differently to ensure credits paid back to data centers do not shift costs onto other wholesale customers.
quotes: An appropriate crediting mechanism for this purpose will need to be different from the crediting mechanism used for large generator interconnections.
